entries.content:
Device CategoryDevice ExamplesKey Technical FeaturesRecanalization RatesClinical OutcomesSafety ProfileReferences**Balloon Guide Catheters (BGC)**FlowGate, Merci, Cello• Inflatable balloon at tip for proximal flow arrest
• Enables flow reversal during retrieval
• Allows aspiration through lumenVariable impact on final TICI 2b-3 ratesMeta-analyses: OR 1.48 for good functional outcome (mRS 0-2)
**However**: PROTECT-MT trial showed **worse** 90-day outcomes vs conventional guide catheters• More vasospasm (device-related)
• Longer procedure time in some studies
• No difference in sICH or mortality[1], [2], [3]**Stent Retrievers****Solitaire FR/Platinum**, **Trevo ProVue/XP**• Self-expanding nitinol mesh
• Deployed across thrombus
• Solitaire: 4-6mm diameter, 20-40mm length
• Trevo: 3-6mm diameter, 20-30mm length**TICI 2b-3**: 82-90%
**First-pass effect**: Higher with Trevo in some studies
Solitaire 68% vs Merci 30% (SWIFT)
Trevo 86% vs Merci 60% (TREVO2)**mRS 0-2 at 90 days**: 36-40% vs 22-29% with Merci
Trevo showed shorter procedure time and fewer passes vs Solitaire in some studies**sICH**: 2-3%
**Mortality**: 14-17%
Lower hemorrhage rates than first-generation devices[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9]**Contact Aspiration Catheters****Standard bore**: Penumbra 5MAX (0.054"), ACE 64 (0.064")
**Large bore**: ACE 68 (0.068"), Esperance (0.071")
**Super large bore**: HiPoint (0.088"), Vecta (0.082")• Direct aspiration first pass technique (ADAPT)
• Tracked to clot face
• Manual or pump aspiration
• Larger ID = higher aspiration force**Standard bore**: TICI 2b-3 77-82%
**Large bore (≥0.068")**: TICI 2b-3 89-92%, FPE 43-65%
**Super large bore**: FPE ~55%, final TICI 2c-3 81%**ASTER/COMPASS**: Non-inferior to stent retrievers for mRS 0-2
Large bore: 42-62% mRS 0-2 at 90 days
Larger catheters = shorter procedure time**sICH**: 3.8-4%
Catheters ≥0.068" associated with higher hemorrhage rates
Arterial perforation: 1.3%[10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17], [18]**Combined Technique (Solumbra)**Stent retriever + aspiration catheter• Stent retriever deployed across thrombus
• Aspiration catheter at proximal thrombus
• Withdrawn together under aspiration
• Often with BGC ("BADDASS" technique)**TICI 2b-3**: Similar to stent retriever alone
**FPE**: 40.8% in some seriesNon-inferior to individual techniques
May improve first-pass successSimilar safety profile to individual techniques[12], [19], [20]**Rescue/Adjunct Stents****Wingspan**, **Enterprise**, **Credo** (for ICAS)• Self-expanding intracranial stents
• Used for underlying stenosis or failed thrombectomy
• Requires antiplatelet therapy**TIMI 2-3**: 92-100% when used as rescue
RESCUE-ICAS: mTICI ≥2b in 90.9% with stenting vs 77.9% without**RESCUE-ICAS**: 42.2% mRS 0-2 with stenting vs 28.4% without (aOR 2.67)
Historical series: 42-60% good outcome**sICH**: 9-16%
Higher complication rate (12.6% vs 5%)
Mortality: 14-33% in early series[21], [22], [23], [24], [25]

## Key Findings Across Device Categories

**Stent Retrievers vs Aspiration**: The ASTER and COMPASS trials demonstrated **non-inferiority** between contact aspiration and stent retrievers for first-line thrombectomy, with similar recanalization rates (85% vs 83%) and functional outcomes.[10][11][12] Both techniques are now considered acceptable first-line approaches.[19]

Figure 2
Modified Treatment in Cerebral Infarction (mTICI) Scores at the End of All Endovascular Procedures and After First-Line Strategy Alone
undefined

**Impact of Aspiration Catheter Size**: Meta-regression analysis shows that **larger aspiration catheters** (ID ≥0.064") are associated with higher first-pass effect (p=0.032), better final recanalization (p=0.002), and reduced need for rescue therapy (p=0.013).[14] However, catheters ≥0.068" show increased hemorrhagic complications (p=0.025).[14] The recent SUMMIT MAX trial demonstrated that super large bore catheters (0.088") achieved 77.5% successful reperfusion vs 50.6% with standard large bore (0.082"), though 90-day functional outcomes were similar (50.6% vs 53.3% mRS 0-2).[13]

**Balloon Guide Catheters - Controversial**: While meta-analyses of observational data suggested benefits (OR 1.48 for good outcomes, reduced distal emboli), the[2][3] **2024 PROTECT-MT randomized trial** unexpectedly showed **worse functional recovery** with BGC vs conventional guide catheters, with longer procedure times and more vasospasm.[1] This trial was terminated early and had high rates of intracranial atherosclerosis, limiting generalizability.[1]

Figure 2
Forest Plot Meta-Analysis
Marmagkiolis K, Hakeem A, Cilingiroglu M, et al. Safety and Efficacy of Stent Retrievers for the Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke: Comprehensive Review and Meta-Analysis. JACC. Cardiovascular Interventions. 2015;8(13):1758-65. doi:10.1016/j.jcin.2015.07.021.

Figure 2
Primary and Key Secondary End Point Differences Between Treatment Groups
Nogueira RG, Frei D, Kirmani JF, et al. Safety and Efficacy of a 3-Dimensional Stent Retriever With Aspiration-Based Thrombectomy vs Aspiration-Based Thrombectomy Alone in Acute Ischemic Stroke Intervention: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Neurology. 2018;75(3):304-311. doi:10.1001/jamaneurol.2017.3967.

Figure 5
Endovascular Stroke Treatment
Papanagiotou P, White CJ. Endovascular Reperfusion Strategies for Acute Stroke. JACC. Cardiovascular Interventions. 2016;9(4):307-317. doi:10.1016/j.jcin.2015.11.014.

Figure 1
Functional Outcomes at 90 Days, According to the Score on the Modified Rankin Scale.
Saver JL, Goyal M, Bonafe A, et al. Stent-Retriever Thrombectomy After Intravenous T-Pa vs. T-Pa Alone in Stroke. The New England Journal of Medicine. 2015;372(24):2285-95. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1415061.

Figure 5
Time to Intervention, TICI 2b/3 Rate, and NNT
Khandelwal P, Yavagal DR, Sacco RL. Acute Ischemic Stroke Intervention. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2016;67(22):2631-44. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2016.03.555.

**Combined Techniques**: The "Solumbra" technique (stent retriever + aspiration) and "BADDASS" approach (Balloon guide + Distal Access catheter + Dual Aspiration + Stent retriever) are increasingly used, with first-pass effect rates of approximately 40-65%.[12][20] These combined approaches may synergize benefits but require further randomized evaluation.[19]

**Rescue Stenting for ICAS**: The 2025 RESCUE-ICAS registry showed that emergent intracranial stenting for underlying stenosis after failed thrombectomy significantly improved functional independence (42.2% vs 28.4%, aOR 2.67) without increased symptomatic hemorrhage, though overall complication rates were higher.[23]

Would you like me to explore the specific technical considerations for device selection based on clot location and characteristics, or discuss emerging technologies like adjustable stent retrievers and automated aspiration systems?

### References

1. Balloon Guide Catheters for Endovascular Thrombectomy in Patients With Acute Ischaemic Stroke Due to Large-Vessel Occlusion in China (PROTECT-MT): A Multicentre, Open-Label, Blinded-Endpoint, Randomised Controlled Trial. Liu J, Zhou Y, Zhang L, et al. Lancet (London, England). 2024;404(10468):2165-2174. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(24)02315-8.
2. Clinical and Procedural Outcomes With or Without Balloon Guide Catheters During Endovascular Thrombectomy in Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis With First-Line Technique Subgroup Analysis. Podlasek A, Dhillon PS, Jewett G, et al. AJNR. American Journal of Neuroradiology. 2021;42(8):1464-1471. doi:10.3174/ajnr.A7164.
3. Comparison of Balloon Guide Catheters and Standard Guide Catheters for Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Pederson JM, Reierson NL, Hardy N, et al. World Neurosurgery. 2021;154:144-153.e21. doi:10.1016/j.wneu.2021.07.034.
4. Indications for the Performance of Intracranial Endovascular Neurointerventional Procedures: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association. Eskey CJ, Meyers PM, Nguyen TN, et al. Circulation. 2018;137(21):e661-e689. doi:10.1161/CIR.0000000000000567.
5. Comparison of the Solitaire and Trevo Stents for Endovascular Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Single.Center Experience. Baek JW, Heo YJ, Kim ST, et al. Neurology India. 2021 Mar-Apr;69(2):378-382. doi:10.4103/0028-3886.314580.
6. Effectiveness of Trevo Stent Retriever in Acute Ischemic Stroke: Comparison With Solitaire Stent. Yi HJ, Lee DH, Kim SU. Medicine. 2018;97(20):e10747. doi:10.1097/MD.0000000000010747.
7. Comparison of Four Food and Drug Administration-Approved Mechanical Thrombectomy Devices for Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Network Meta-Analysis. Deng L, Qiu S, Wang L, et al. World Neurosurgery. 2019;127:e49-e57. doi:10.1016/j.wneu.2019.02.011.
8. Comparison of Non-Stent Retriever and Stent Retriever Mechanical Thrombectomy Devices for the Endovascular Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke. Hentschel KA, Daou B, Chalouhi N, et al. Journal of Neurosurgery. 2017;126(4):1123-1130. doi:10.3171/2016.2.JNS152086.
9. Safety and Efficacy of Stent Retrievers for the Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke: Comprehensive Review and Meta-Analysis. Marmagkiolis K, Hakeem A, Cilingiroglu M, et al. JACC. Cardiovascular Interventions. 2015;8(13):1758-65. doi:10.1016/j.jcin.2015.07.021.
10. Safety and Efficacy of a 3-Dimensional Stent Retriever With Aspiration-Based Thrombectomy vs Aspiration-Based Thrombectomy Alone in Acute Ischemic Stroke Intervention: A Randomized Clinical Trial. Nogueira RG, Frei D, Kirmani JF, et al. JAMA Neurology. 2018;75(3):304-311. doi:10.1001/jamaneurol.2017.3967.
11. Effect of Endovascular Contact Aspiration vs Stent Retriever on Revascularization in Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke and Large Vessel Occlusion: The ASTER Randomized Clinical Trial. Lapergue B, Blanc R, Gory B, et al. JAMA. 2017;318(5):443-452. doi:10.1001/jama.2017.9644.
12. Hyperacute Management of Ischemic Strokes: JACC Focus Seminar. Patel P, Yavagal D, Khandelwal P. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2020;75(15):1844-1856. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2020.03.006.
13. SUMMIT MAX: A Randomized Trial of the Super Large Bore HiPoint Reperfusion System Versus Vecta System for Aspiration Thrombectomy. Nguyen TN, Dabus G, McGuinness B, et al. Stroke. 2025;56(8):1980-1990. doi:10.1161/STROKEAHA.125.051742.
14. Impact of Aspiration Catheter Size on Clinical Outcomes in Aspiration Thrombectomy. Schartz D, Ellens N, Kohli GS, et al. Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery. 2023;15(e1):e111-e116. doi:10.1136/jnis-2022-019246.
15. A Prospective, Multicenter, Single-Group Target-Value Clinical Trial to Evaluate the Safety and Efficacy of a Large Bore Aspiration Catheter System for the Endovascular Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke. Chen F, Bai X, Hu W, et al. Frontiers in Neurology. 2022;13:864563. doi:10.3389/fneur.2022.864563.
16. Lessons Learned Over More Than 500 Stroke Thrombectomies Using ADAPT With Increasing Aspiration Catheter Size. Alawieh A, Chatterjee AR, Vargas J, et al. Neurosurgery. 2020;86(1):61-70. doi:10.1093/neuros/nyy444.
17. Super Large Bore Catheters in the Treatment of Stroke: Systematic Review. Jabłońska M, Ribo M. Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery. 2026;:jnis-2026-025001. doi:10.1136/jnis-2026-025001.
18. Impact of Increasing Aspiration Catheter Size and Refinement of Technique: Experience of Over 1000 Strokes Treated With ADAPT. Al Kasab S, Almallouhi E, Alawieh A, et al. Neurosurgery. 2022;91(1):80-86. doi:10.1227/neu.0000000000001937.
19. Different Types of Percutaneous Endovascular Interventions for Acute Ischemic Stroke. Bai X, Zhang X, Gong H, et al. The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews. 2023;5:CD014676. doi:10.1002/14651858.CD014676.pub2.
20. Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke Due to Large-Vessel Occlusion: JACC Focus Seminar. Ospel JM, Holodinsky JK, Goyal M. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2020;75(15):1832-1843. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2019.10.034.
21. Stroke Intervention: Catheter-Based Therapy for Acute Ischemic Stroke. White CJ, Abou-Chebl A, Cates CU, et al. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2011;58(2):101-16. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2011.02.049.
22. Stent Placement in Acute Cerebral Artery Occlusion: Use of a Self-Expandable Intracranial Stent for Acute Stroke Treatment. Brekenfeld C, Schroth G, Mattle HP, et al. Stroke. 2009;40(3):847-52. doi:10.1161/STROKEAHA.108.533810.
23. Outcomes of Adjunct Emergent Stenting Versus Mechanical Thrombectomy Alone: The RESCUE-ICAS Registry. Al Kasab S, Almallouhi E, Jumaa M, et al. Stroke. 2025;56(2):390-400. doi:10.1161/STROKEAHA.124.049038.
24. Initial Experience With a Self-Expanding Retrievable Stent for Recanalization of Large Vessel Occlusions in Acute Ischemic Stroke. Menon BK, Kochar P, Ah-Seng A, et al. Neuroradiology. 2012;54(2):147-54. doi:10.1007/s00234-010-0835-x.
25. Self-Expandable Stents in the Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke Refractory to Current Thrombectomy Devices. Linfante I, Samaniego EA, Geisbüsch P, Dabus G. Stroke. 2011;42(9):2636-8. doi:10.1161/STROKEAHA.111.618389.
26. Endovascular Reperfusion Strategies for Acute Stroke. Papanagiotou P, White CJ. JACC. Cardiovascular Interventions. 2016;9(4):307-317. doi:10.1016/j.jcin.2015.11.014.
27. Stent-Retriever Thrombectomy after Intravenous t-PA vs. t-PA Alone in Stroke. Saver JL, Goyal M, Bonafe A, et al. The New England Journal of Medicine. 2015;372(24):2285-95. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1415061.
28. Acute Ischemic Stroke Intervention. Khandelwal P, Yavagal DR, Sacco RL. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2016;67(22):2631-44. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2016.03.555.

<Uptodate>

You are asking how to evaluate an 80-year-old female with thunderclap headache for 3 hours followed by dizziness and unsteady gait. I am assuming the patient is currently symptomatic and this is a nontraumatic presentation.
Is there anything to add or change? 
 
This presentation should be treated as a neurologic emergency because thunderclap headache requires immediate evaluation for subarachnoid hemorrhage and other secondary causes, and acute gait ataxia raises concern for posterior circulation stroke or cerebellar hemorrhage.
+2
Immediate priorities
Proceed with urgent neuroimaging and parallel neurologic assessment rather than symptomatic treatment alone.
+1
First-line imaging and SAH rule-out pathway
Obtain noncontrast head CT immediately to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage and other causes (including ischemic stroke).
+3
If CT is negative, lumbar puncture is recommended in most cases to evaluate CSF for subarachnoid hemorrhage, particularly when CT is performed more than 6 hours after onset.
+3
Lumbar puncture may be omitted only in select patients when a technically adequate CT is obtained within 6 hours of onset and read as negative by an experienced radiologist, with appropriate caveats.
+2
Neurovascular imaging for alternative causes and posterior circulation process
If the cause remains uncertain after initial SAH evaluation, obtain additional brain and neurovascular imaging, with brain MRI favored when available.
+1
Include MR venography or CT venography to evaluate for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis in patients presenting with thunderclap headache.
+1
Given dizziness and gait ataxia, maintain high suspicion for posterior circulation ischemic stroke and pursue neuroimaging accordingly; MRI is often needed when CT is unrevealing and suspicion remains high.
+2
Focused bedside assessment elements that change pretest probability
Assess for features associated with higher odds of subarachnoid hemorrhage (eg, impaired consciousness, neck stiffness, nausea/vomiting, exertional onset, elevated blood pressure, occipital location).
A clearly abnormal neurologic examination or severely ataxic gait supports a central process and warrants neuroimaging and neurologic evaluation.
+1

_comment: EBM notes pushed from the PHCEP app for AI conversion to 衛教資源. Each entry must have: id, date, content (≥4 non-empty lines). The convert-ebm-to-edu workflow processes this file and clears it after success.
